title: Chapter 34: Sunburn
content: Maya's fingers closed around his wrist.
Three inches from the kill switch panel — and her hand was on him, gripping, pulling, desperate. But the thing she felt stopped her cold.
His skin was *cold*.

Not cool. Not the chill of a man who'd been standing under air conditioning. This was the cold of a body pulled from ice — a deep, structural absence of warmth, as if blood hadn't moved beneath that skin in centuries. As if there *was* no blood. Her fingers registered it before her brain did: the wrongness of it, the way his pulse should have been there and simply *wasn't*, the way his tendons felt like cables under refrigerated hide.
Vlad turned to look at her.
His eyes met hers, and Maya's stomach dropped through the floor.

They were not human eyes. They had never been human eyes. They were ancient and empty and *starving* — the eyes of something that had been hungry for so long that hunger had become its only identity. They held no warmth either. No recognition. No mercy. They looked at her the way a furnace looks at fuel.
"You should have taken my offer," he whispered.
His other hand came up — casually, almost gently — and shoved her.

Maya flew backward. Not stumbled. Not fell. *Flew.* As if she weighed nothing. As if she were a piece of paper caught in a wind that shouldn't exist inside a building. Her back hit the base of the podium with a crack that shot through her spine and drove the air from her lungs. She crumpled onto the marble floor, gasping, the copper taste of blood blooming on her tongue where she'd bitten through her lip on impact.
Across the NYSE floor, traders were standing frozen, staring at the screens. No one moved. No one could move.
Vlad slammed his palm against the kill switch panel.
Nothing happened.

He pressed again. *Nothing.* The button was dead — the circuit bypassed, rerouted, cauterized by the hard-line injection Chloe had fed into the system forty seconds ago. The broadcast didn't skip. It didn't stutter. Lucy Chen's voice kept pouring from every speaker in the building, clear and steady and unkillable:
"—they took blood from me every night for thirty-one days. They called it 'processing.' I call it what it is—a farm."

Vlad stared at the dead panel. Then at the screens. Then something shifted in his face — a rearrangement so fundamental it was like watching tectonic plates move. The composed CEO, the measured billionaire, the man who had smiled at talk shows and charmed regulators and built an empire of polished lies — all of it *fell away*. What remained was a mask of absolute rage. Something that hadn't surfaced in centuries. Something that had been buried so deep it had forgotten what daylight felt like.
He turned.
Marcus was standing in the wings. Stock-still. Hands at his sides. Watching.
"FIX THIS."

Vlad's voice wasn't a request. It wasn't even a command. It was a compulsion — a frequency that should have driven Marcus's body into motion whether he wanted it to or not. Five hundred years of obedience encoded in a single syllable.
Marcus didn't move.

He stood perfectly still. He met Vlad's eyes — those ancient, starving, terrible eyes — and he said nothing. His jaw was set. His hands didn't tremble. Five hundred years of servitude, and Marcus Webb chose this moment, this second, this breath, to simply *stop*.

Vlad's face contorted. For the first time — perhaps for the first time in half a millennium — something besides hunger and rage crossed his features. Something that might have been confusion. Or loss. Or the faintest precursor to fear.
On the screens, the evidence kept playing.

Sublevel B blueprints, detailed and damning — the holding cells, the extraction chairs, the drainage systems designed with the clinical precision of a slaughterhouse. Lumina app screenshots, code comments and all, showing the biometric harvesting architecture embedded beneath the ovulation tracker. Dutch-language records from Verwey Medical, 1987–2003, documenting "long-term hemoglobin accumulation studies." Specimen viability metrics parsed into clinical spreadsheets — women reduced to rows and columns, their fertility indexed, their blood typed and rated. Kapoor's emails, the ones Maya had found on that encrypted drive weeks ago, now broadcast in plain text to the world.
And then —
The 4.2 million phones.

Chloe's Plan B. The one she'd built in silence, the one she'd never mentioned to anyone because mentioning it would have been a confession of what she was willing to do. She'd found the exploit in Lumina's push notification system three days ago and had sat on it like a land mine, waiting. Now she detonated it.

Across America, across the world, 4.2 million women who had Lumina installed on their phones — who trusted it with their cycle data, their fertility windows, their most intimate biological rhythms — watched their wellness apps glitch and rewrite themselves. The soft pink interface vanished. In its place: Lucy Chen's face. Not the composed, filter-smoothed face from the Lumina marketing materials. The real face. Gaunt. Scarred. Eyes that had seen the inside of Sublevel B.
And beneath it, in white text on a black field:
**THIS APP IS USED TO HARVEST YOUR BLOOD. DELETE IT NOW. #BLOODHARVEST**
Four point two million screens. Four point two million witnesses.
In the NYSE, the press pool went mad.

Every journalist with a credential was shooting — cameras up, shutters firing, flash after flash after flash detonating in the cavernous space like artillery. The light was staggering, a strobing wall of white that turned the trading floor into a disco of revelation. Maya, still on the floor, could see the pulses through her half-closed eyes — each flash painting Vlad's frozen silhouette in stark relief against the cracked screens behind him.
But something was wrong with the flashes.
They weren't white. Not anymore.

Maya blinked. The light had shifted — it was harsher now, sharper, carrying an edge her eyes couldn't quite resolve. The color was wrong. The *frequency* was wrong. It was reaching past her retinas into something deeper, something her skin could feel — a prickling, ionizing tension, like standing too close to a tanning bed cranked to maximum.

In the production truck outside, Chloe Park's fingers were flying across her keyboard. The press pool's wireless flash control system — modern networked units synced for remote synchronization, the kind every major outlet used for coordinated event coverage — had a flaw. Not a bug. A feature. The firmware allowed spectrum adjustment for color temperature correction. It was meant for indoor-outdoor calibration. Chloe had overridden every unit in the room to output at maximum intensity in the ultraviolet range. Not visible light. UV. 280 to 400 nanometers. *Sunlight equivalent.*
Every camera flash in the NYSE had become a burst of simulated sun.
And Vlad was standing directly beneath them.

The first flash — he flinched. The second — his hand went to his cheek. The third, the fourth, the tenth, the fiftieth — they were coming from everywhere now, hundreds of journalists firing in cascading waves, and each burst was a needle of UV radiation punching into his skin.
His cheek reddened.

Not a blush. Not embarrassment. *Sunburn.* Severe, immediate, impossible — human skin didn't react that fast. But whatever was wearing Alexander Vlad's face was not human, and it had not seen sunlight in five hundred years. The redness deepened to crimson. Then to something darker. Blistering — rapid, violent, the skin rising in welts and rupturing before Maya's eyes. She could *smell* it now. Burning. Not like cooking meat — nothing that organic. This was sulfur and copper, the smell of electrical fires and old blood, the smell of something that should have been buried long ago being forced into the light.
The skin on his face began to char.

It peeled in sheets — not peeling away to reveal muscle or tendon or bone, but peeling away to reveal something *older*. Something dead. The texture beneath the sloughing dermis was grey and papery, like parchment left in a tomb. The cheekbones were wrong — too high, too sharp, the architecture of a skull that predated modern anatomy. The jawline was asymmetrical in a way that no cosmetic surgeon would have left, because no cosmetic surgeon could have built it. It was the scaffolding of something that had been constructed, not grown. Something that had *worn* faces before Alexander Vlad's, and would have worn others after.

The creature that had hidden behind five hundred years of smiles and handshakes and magazine covers was being unmasked in real time, on every screen, for every viewer. The internet was already burning. Every frame was being captured, clipped, shared, analyzed. There was no containing this. There was no spinning this. There was no deleting this.
The mask fell, and the world saw what was underneath.
Vlad *screamed*.
It was not a human sound.

It was a frequency — a vibration that had nothing to do with vocal cords or breath or the architecture of a human throat. It came from somewhere deeper, somewhere older, somewhere that had been silent for centuries and had forgotten how to be quiet gently. The sound hit the walls of the NYSE and *resonated*. Maya felt it in her sternum before she heard it in her ears — a subsonic pulse that made her rib cage vibrate and her teeth ache.
0.7 hertz.

She knew that number. She'd found it in Lucy's podcast audio, weeks ago, that impossible frequency running beneath a dead woman's breathing pattern like a hidden track. She'd spent hours trying to identify it, running it through filters, analyzing its waveform, cataloging every possible source. She'd thought it was interference. She'd thought it was equipment malfunction. She'd thought it was breathing.
*It was never breathing.*

Every pane of glass in the NYSE cracked. Simultaneously. The sound was a single, unified report — CRACK — as if the building itself had flinched. The great windows along the facade splintered into spiderwebs of fractured light. The protective glass over the screens fissured and crazed. Traders collapsed, hands over their ears, some of them crawling toward exits, others simply curling on the floor in the fetal position. The sound was inside them and outside them and it was *everywhere*.

Outside on Broad Street, birds dropped from the sky. Pigeons that had been perched on ledges simply folded, their tiny hearts stuttering under the subsonic assault. The sound traveled through concrete and steel, through the bones of the building, through the very air.

Maya was on the floor, headphones clutched to her ears, and yet the sound bypassed everything — bypassed her headphones, bypassed her ear canals, resonated directly through her skull. She tasted copper and sulfur. She felt the marble vibrating against her cheek. And in the eye of that terrible storm, she understood:
*That's what I heard.*

*That's what Lucy heard in that room on Sublevel B. The breathing pattern I analyzed — the 0.7Hz anomaly — it was never breathing. It was his voice. His true voice. The voice he uses when he's not pretending to be something he's not.*
She'd been listening to a monster try to speak human and fail. Lucy had heard the real thing. And now, finally, so did she.

The scream tapered off into a sound like tearing metal, and then silence — a silence so total it felt like pressure, like the air itself had been evacuated and replaced with something heavy and waiting.
Vlad's face was half-gone.

Half beautiful CEO — the left side, mostly intact, the cheek still flushed with borrowed vitality, the eye still sharp, the jaw still carrying the confident line that had graced a hundred magazine covers. Half ancient corpse — the right side a ruin of peeling skin and grey, papery tissue, the cheekbone jutting like a cliff face, the eye a dark hole that held something worse than blindness. He staggered backward from the podium, hands rising to cover what was left of his face, and the cameras *loved* it — every lens locked on the disintegration, every feed carrying the image to every corner of the connected world. The broadcast was still live. The screens still showed Lucy's evidence on loop. 4.2 million phones still blared their #BLOODHARVEST warning. In London, Jordi's contact at the *Guardian* hit publish. In Berlin, *Der Spiegel* went live. In Tokyo, the *Yomiuri Shimbun* pushed the full story — source documents, translated emails, everything — to their homepage. Three continents. Simultaneous. Irreversible.

The truth was out. It was in the air now, in the fiber optics and the satellite uplinks and the 5G towers and the millions of screens that had been waiting for something real. It couldn't be contained. It couldn't be killed-switched. It couldn't be buried in Sublevel B with the bodies and the blood and the silence.
Vlad lowered his hands.

His remaining eye found Maya. She was still on the floor — bruised, bleeding from her lip, headphones dangling from one hand, staring up at him with the horrified comprehension of someone who has just watched the laws of physics renegotiate themselves.
His eye held nothing but hatred. Pure, distilled, ancient hatred. The kind that remembered faces. The kind that waited.
He moved toward her.

One step. Two. His gait was wrong — too smooth, too fluid, the motion of something that didn't need joints or tendons the way humans did. The half of his face that was gone didn't move at all. The half that remained twisted into something that might have been a smile on a creature that had never learned what smiles were for.
Three steps. He was close enough now that she could smell him — not the cologne, not the wood-and-leather scent he wore like costume, but what lay beneath it. Sulfur. Copper. The grave.
Four steps.
Marcus stepped between them.

He didn't run. He didn't shout. He stepped — one fluid motion, placing his body in the narrow space between the ancient thing wearing Alexander Vlad's half-face and the woman on the floor. He stood squarely, shoulders back, feet planted. His hands were at his sides. Unclenched. Steady.
Vlad stopped.

The two men faced each other. The master and the servant. The parasite and the Renfield. Five hundred years of obedience, of carrying bags and cleaning messes and looking the other way, of learning not to see what he saw and knowing what he knew — five hundred years compressed into this single moment of stillness.
Marcus looked into the ruined face. The human half. The inhuman half. He looked at both equally, because both were real, and both were the same creature, and he had served them both long enough.
"No."
One word.

It wasn't loud. It wasn't dramatic. Marcus didn't shout it or snarl it or weep it. He simply said it — the way a door closes, the way a period ends a sentence, the way five centuries of complicity finally collapse under the weight of their own impossibility.

Vlad stared at him. The hatred in his remaining eye flickered — not diminishing, but *complicating*. There was something else there now. Something that looked almost like confusion. As if the word didn't compute. As if no one had ever said it before. As if, in five hundred years of absolute dominion, no one had ever simply refused.
Marcus didn't blink.
He stood between the monster and his ally, and he held the line, and the cameras rolled, and the world watched, and the silence held — fragile as cracked glass, stubborn as bone.
